(originally known as **rogue folk**)[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a fusion of [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") and [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock"), particularly characterized by a folk-inspired sound blended with punk-inspired lyrics. It was popularized in the early 1980s by [the Pogues](/wiki/The_Pogues "The Pogues") in England, and by [Violent Femmes](/wiki/Violent_Femmes "Violent Femmes") in the United States. Folk punk achieved some mainstream success in that decade. In more recent years, its subgenres [Celtic punk](/wiki/Celtic_punk "Celtic punk") and [gypsy punk](/wiki/Gypsy_punk "Gypsy punk") have experienced some commercial success.

Fusion of folk music and punk rock

**Folk punk** (originally known as **rogue folk**)[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a fusion of [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") and [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock"), particularly characterized by a folk-inspired sound blended with punk-inspired lyrics. It was popularized in the early 1980s by [the Pogues](/wiki/The_Pogues "The Pogues") in England, and by [Violent Femmes](/wiki/Violent_Femmes "Violent Femmes") in the United States. Folk punk achieved some mainstream success in that decade. In more recent years, its subgenres [Celtic punk](/wiki/Celtic_punk "Celtic punk") and [gypsy punk](/wiki/Gypsy_punk "Gypsy punk") have experienced some commercial success.

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_punk&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

Folk punk is related to and/or influenced by various styles such as [Celtic punk](/wiki/Celtic_punk "Celtic punk"), [gypsy punk](/wiki/Gypsy_punk "Gypsy punk"), [anti-folk](/wiki/Anti-folk "Anti-folk"), and [alternative country](/wiki/Alternative_country "Alternative country"). Folk punk is also linked with [DIY punk](/wiki/DIY_ethic "DIY ethic") scenes, and bands often perform in [house venues](/wiki/House_concert "House concert") in addition to more traditional spaces.[[2]](#cite_note-Haas2013-2)

Folk-punk musicians may perform their own compositions in the style of punk rock, but using additional folk instruments, such as [mandolins](/wiki/Mandolin "Mandolin"), [accordions](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion"), [banjos](/wiki/Banjo "Banjo"), and/or [violins](/wiki/Violin "Violin").[[3]](#cite_note-Sweers2005-3) Folk punk possesses a rich history of progressive and leftist political views, involving topics like race, class, feminism, anti-fascism, animal rights, queerness, and anarchism.[[2]](#cite_note-Haas2013-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_punk&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Precursors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_punk&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Precursors")]

The earliest known relationship between rebellious folk music and the ethos that would later define punk rock is that of [Woody Guthrie](/wiki/Woody_Guthrie "Woody Guthrie"), often known as a precursor to the [punk ethos](/wiki/Punk_ethos "Punk ethos"), who sung songs about [anti-fascism](/wiki/Anti-fascism "Anti-fascism") and the conditions faced by [working-class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") people, starting in the 1930s.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) In the 1960s, [New York's folk music revival scene](/wiki/American_folk_music_revival "American folk music revival") spearheaded by artists like [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") would birth other early precursors to folk punk which included underground [proto-punk](/wiki/Proto-punk "Proto-punk") artists like [David Peel](/wiki/David_Peel_(musician) "David Peel (musician)"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) [the Godz](/wiki/The_Godz_(New_York_band) "The Godz (New York band)"), [the Holy Modal Rounders](/wiki/The_Holy_Modal_Rounders "The Holy Modal Rounders")[[9]](#cite_note-9) and [the Fugs](/wiki/The_Fugs "The Fugs")[[10]](#cite_note-10) whose style of crude vulgar folk music would later prove influential to [anti-folk](/wiki/Anti-folk "Anti-folk") artists like [Jeffrey Lewis](/wiki/Jeffrey_Lewis "Jeffrey Lewis").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_punk&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1970s")]

In 1977 [London](/wiki/London "London")-born singer-songwriter [Patrik Fitzgerald](/wiki/Patrik_Fitzgerald "Patrik Fitzgerald") released his first EP titled *Safety-Pin Stuck in My Heart* which was subtitled "a love song for punk music". The titular song from the EP still remains Fitzgerald's most famous work and acted as one of the pioneering releases for folk punk by combining punk rock imagery with acoustic guitar and vocals.

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_punk&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1980s")]

Formed in [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee") in 1981, [Violent Femmes](/wiki/Violent_Femmes "Violent Femmes") was one of the first and most commercially successful bands to fuse punk and folk, though much of their influence came more from early [art rock](/wiki/Art_rock "Art rock") acts like the Velvet Underground.[[12]](#cite_note-12) During the 1980s other punk and hardcore bands would pepper their albums with acoustic tracks or inject folksier sounds, notably [the Dead Milkmen](/wiki/The_Dead_Milkmen "The Dead Milkmen") and [Articles of Faith](/wiki/Articles_of_Faith_(band) "Articles of Faith (band)"). An influential album was the punk-inflected folk-country album released in 1984 when psychedelic hardcore band [the Meat Puppets](/wiki/The_Meat_Puppets "The Meat Puppets") switched their style for their seminal release *[Meat Puppets II](/wiki/Meat_Puppets_II "Meat Puppets II")*.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In the UK, the fusion of folk and punk was pioneered by the London-based Irish band [the Pogues](/wiki/The_Pogues "The Pogues"), formed in 1982, whose mixture of original songs and covers of established folk singers, many performed in a punk style, led to three top ten albums in the UK, a number two single in "[Fairytale of New York](/wiki/Fairytale_of_New_York "Fairytale of New York")" (1987) with [Kirsty McColl](/wiki/Kirsty_McColl "Kirsty McColl"), and a string of top ten singles and albums in Ireland.[[14]](#cite_note-Buckley2003-14) The Pogues' lead singer [Shane MacGowan](/wiki/Shane_MacGowan "Shane MacGowan") had played in London punk outfit [the Nips](/wiki/The_Nips "The Nips"), originally known as the Nipple Erectors.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The pioneers of a more distinctively English brand of folk punk were [the Men They Couldn't Hang](/wiki/The_Men_They_Couldn%27t_Hang "The Men They Couldn't Hang"), founded in 1984. Also important was the [Oysterband](/wiki/Oysterband "Oysterband"), who developed from playing English [Céilidh](/wiki/C%C3%A9ilidh "Céilidh") music with a fast and harder rock sound from around 1986.[[3]](#cite_note-Sweers2005-3) [The Levellers](/wiki/The_Levellers_(band) "The Levellers (band)"), founded in 1988, made less use of traditional melodies but more use of acoustic instruments, including violins.[[3]](#cite_note-Sweers2005-3) Several other prominent members of the English punk scene in the early 1980s were also experimenting with folk influences. Early demos by [Chumbawamba](/wiki/Chumbawamba "Chumbawamba") feature the accordion and the trumpet, though it would take them over 20 years to transition into a full-fledged folk act. [Attila the Stockbroker](/wiki/Attila_the_Stockbroker "Attila the Stockbroker") began entertaining punk audiences accompanied by [mandola](/wiki/Mandola "Mandola") in 1986, and is still performing. Probably the most successful figure associated with English 1980s folk punk is singer-songwriter [Billy Bragg](/wiki/Billy_Bragg "Billy Bragg"), who enjoyed a series of hits in the 1980s and became a distinct influence on later folk punk acts.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_punk&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1990s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Danbert2.jpg/250px-Danbert2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Danbert2.jpg)

Chumbawamba vocalist [Danbert Nobacon](/wiki/Danbert_Nobacon "Danbert Nobacon") pictured playing live at the [University of Leeds](/wiki/University_of_Leeds "University of Leeds"), 1986, supporting [Conflict](/wiki/Conflict_(band) "Conflict (band)").

The early nineties saw a general lull of interest in folk-influenced punk, but there were a few acts touring. Formed in 1990, Austin-based experimental bluegrass band [Bad Livers](/wiki/Bad_Livers "Bad Livers") is one example, though the band used punk more as a cultural reference point than a musical one, often appearing wearing [Misfits](/wiki/Misfits_(band) "Misfits (band)") shirts and occasionally covering [Iggy Pop's](/wiki/Iggy_Pop "Iggy Pop") "Lust for Life".[[17]](#cite_note-17)

1994 saw the creation of [Plan-It-X Records](/wiki/Plan-It-X_Records "Plan-It-X Records").[[18]](#cite_note-planitx-18) which would later go on to release some of the most quintessential folk punk acts of the late 90s/early 2000s[[19]](#cite_note-19) and have a large influence on the genre.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Another genre-defining act, [the Moldy Peaches](/wiki/The_Moldy_Peaches "The Moldy Peaches") formed in 1994 featuring [Adam Green](/wiki/Adam_Green_(musician) "Adam Green (musician)") and [Kimya Dawson](/wiki/Kimya_Dawson "Kimya Dawson"). singing and playing acoustic guitars. The band labeled themselves as [Anti-Folk](/wiki/Anti-folk "Anti-folk").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Formed in 1995, [the World/Inferno Friendship Society](/wiki/The_World/Inferno_Friendship_Society "The World/Inferno Friendship Society") is a large ensemble that came to be influential in the later New York Gypsy Punk scene. They combined elements of [Cabaret](/wiki/Cabaret "Cabaret"), Punk, and [Klezmer](/wiki/Klezmer "Klezmer") into their grandiose and theatrical performances.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

At the close of the 1990s, Celtic punk had a revival as bands like [Dropkick Murphys](/wiki/Dropkick_Murphys "Dropkick Murphys"), [Flogging Molly](/wiki/Flogging_Molly "Flogging Molly"), [Greenland Whalefishers](/wiki/Greenland_Whalefishers "Greenland Whalefishers"), and [the Real McKenzies](/wiki/The_Real_McKenzies "The Real McKenzies") started to experience a wider commercial notice. This wave of bands, who often mixed Pogues-derived sounds with those of [street punk](/wiki/Street_punk "Street punk") bands like [Cockney Rejects](/wiki/Cockney_Rejects "Cockney Rejects"), spawned imitators worldwide.[[14]](#cite_note-Buckley2003-14)

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_punk&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2000s")]

In the early 2000s, a Plan-It-X Records sound, heavily influenced by underground 1990s pop punk and classic [DIY](/wiki/DIY "DIY") ethic began to take shape. For many in the punk community, the record label has become synonymous with folk punk, though they have also released electric acts with little or no folk influence.[[18]](#cite_note-planitx-18) At the same time in New York a [Gypsy Punk](/wiki/Gypsy_Punk "Gypsy Punk") sound began to coalesce around [Gogol Bordello](/wiki/Gogol_Bordello "Gogol Bordello"), [Golem](/wiki/Golem_(klezmer_band) "Golem (klezmer band)"), [Insomniac Folklore](/wiki/Insomniac_Folklore "Insomniac Folklore"), and other groups. These bands took eastern European instrumentation and fused it with the rhythms of punk, ska, and rock. Many of these bands adopted a very recognizable aesthetic influenced by [campy](/wiki/Campy "Campy") references to [Soviet art](/wiki/Socialist_Realism "Socialist Realism"), [burlesque](/wiki/Burlesque "Burlesque") aesthetics, and the classic punk style of [the Clash](/wiki/The_Clash "The Clash"). Gogol Bordello, in particular, achieved a degree of mainstream success.[[22]](#cite_note-NPR-22)

In the Northeastern US, yet another community was emerging around the band [Mischief Brew](/wiki/Mischief_Brew "Mischief Brew") and Fistolo Records.[[23]](#cite_note-23) These acts merged the DIY punk underground with '60s radical folk in the vein of [Phil Ochs](/wiki/Phil_Ochs "Phil Ochs") and contemporary [anarchist](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism") folk musicians like [David Rovics](/wiki/David_Rovics "David Rovics"). Notable artists from the Northeast at this time were punk/[appalachian](/wiki/Appalachian_music "Appalachian music") hybrids [the Can Kickers](/wiki/The_Can_Kickers "The Can Kickers"), and influential singer/songwriter [Pat the Bunny](/wiki/Pat_the_Bunny_(musician) "Pat the Bunny (musician)") under the name [Johnny Hobo and the Freight Trains](/wiki/Johnny_Hobo_and_the_Freight_Trains "Johnny Hobo and the Freight Trains"). Pat later went on to form [Wingnut Dishwashers Union](/wiki/Wingnut_Dishwashers_Union "Wingnut Dishwashers Union") in the late 2000s. He is considered by many to be a highly definitive folk punk artist.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

During this period, the genre gained increased attention as [Against Me!](/wiki/Against_Me! "Against Me!") from [Gainesville, Florida](/wiki/Gainesville,_Florida "Gainesville, Florida") achieved mainstream success; however, this coincided with a shift away from their original folk sound.

In the mid-2000s, the west coast began to produce its own DIY scene of folk punk artists with a different sound, connected with [Santa Cruz's](/wiki/Santa_Cruz,_California "Santa Cruz, California") [Blackbird Raum](/wiki/Blackbird_Raum "Blackbird Raum") who feature a completely acoustic lineup based entirely around traditional instruments, but with fast punk rhythms and bleak, political lyrics influenced by [crust punk](/wiki/Crust_punk "Crust punk"). They are closely associated with the all-acoustic hardcore band [Hail Seizures](/w/index.php?title=Hail_Seizures&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hail Seizures (page does not exist)") and the [Northwest Folklife](/wiki/Northwest_Folklife "Northwest Folklife") festival folk-punk stage.[[25]](#cite_note-25) These west coast bands play acoustically in order to [busk](/wiki/Street_performance "Street performance").[[26]](#cite_note-courant-26) This time also hosted the rise of folk punk created in the Southwest United States. [AJJ](/wiki/AJJ_(band) "AJJ (band)") began in 2004 in [Phoenix, AZ](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona") drawing inspiration from and collaborating with artists around the country.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In 2004, the Moldy Peaches split up and [Kimya Dawson](/wiki/Kimya_Dawson "Kimya Dawson") began releasing solo work. In 2007, her music was featured extensively in the soundtrack for the film *[Juno](/wiki/Juno_(film) "Juno (film)").* This soundtrack went on to win a [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") for Best Compilation Soundtrack in 2009.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In 2006, Brian Sella and Mat Uychich formed [the Front Bottoms](/wiki/The_Front_Bottoms "The Front Bottoms"). Their first two albums, *I Hate My Friends* and *My Grandma vs. Pneumonia*, as well as their first EP, *Brothers Can't Be Friends*, featured them as well as Mat's brother, also named Brian.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### 2010s and 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_punk&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2010s and 2020s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Crass_Pub_Fest_I.jpg/250px-Crass_Pub_Fest_I.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crass_Pub_Fest_I.jpg)

[Anarchist](/wiki/Anarchist "Anarchist") folk punk festival and demonstration

In 2011, Pat the Bunny and others started Ramshackle Glory in [Tucson, Arizona](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This turned out to be his last larger collaborative project, as in 2016, revered by many as an integral part of the community, he announced his retirement from folk punk altogether.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He cited a dramatic change in his viewpoints, shifting away from anarchism in politics and punk music.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

2016 saw also the death of Erik Petersen, member and founder of [Mischief Brew](/wiki/Mischief_Brew "Mischief Brew"), and the subsequent groups disbanding.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

One of the most notable disruptions within the community occurred in 2017 when [Chris Clavin](/wiki/Chris_Clavin "Chris Clavin"), founder of Plan-It-X Records and member of several bands including Ghost Mice, was accused of sexual misconduct by several individuals.[[31]](#cite_note-brooklynvegan-31) While he was defended by some close to him, bands he has collaborated with including [Kimya Dawson](/wiki/Kimya_Dawson "Kimya Dawson"), [AJJ](/wiki/AJJ_(band) "AJJ (band)"), [Waxahatchee](/wiki/Waxahatchee "Waxahatchee"), [Spoonboy](/wiki/The_Max_Levine_Ensemble "The Max Levine Ensemble"), and [Ramshackle Glory](/wiki/Pat_the_Bunny_(musician)#Ramshackle_Glory "Pat the Bunny (musician)") have condemned his actions, left Plan-It-X Records, and removed their work from future printings of split albums.[[31]](#cite_note-brooklynvegan-31)

The continued rise in popularity of several folk-punk acts during this decade was aided by the emergence and spread of musical videos on websites like YouTube. Some of these videos gained widespread recognition, as exemplified by [Days N' Daze](/wiki/Days_N%27_Daze "Days N' Daze")'s Misanthropic Drunken Loner,[[32]](#cite_note-32) reaching well past 8 million views to date. Notably, three channels have emerged over the years, featuring folk punk acts in various formats: A Fistful of Vinyl is a Los Angeles radio show with live sessions every Thursday night on [KXLU](/wiki/KXLU "KXLU") 88.9 FM. AFoV releases videos of their studio sessions since 2012 periodically on YouTube.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Shibby Pictures is a YouTube channel that features mostly indie music videos, short movies and documentaries since about 2010.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The songwriting of [Matt Pless](/w/index.php?title=Matt_Pless&action=edit&redlink=1 "Matt Pless (page does not exist)")'s has been compared to that of Bob Dylan.[[35]](#cite_note-HP2013-35) In 2014 [Bostonian](/wiki/Boston "Boston") working-class folk-punker [Bryan McPherson](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bryan_McPherson "de:Bryan McPherson") got banned by [Disney](/wiki/Disney "Disney") from playing [Anaheim's](/wiki/Anaheim,_California "Anaheim, California") House of Blues, due to his political lyrics.[[36]](#cite_note-DS2014-36)

## Celtic punk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_punk&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Celtic punk")]

Main article: [Celtic punk](/wiki/Celtic_punk "Celtic punk")

The Pogues' style of punked-up Irish music spawned and influenced a number of Celtic punk bands, including [Nyah Fearties](/wiki/Nyah_Fearties "Nyah Fearties") from Scotland, and Australia's [Roaring Jack](/wiki/Roaring_Jack "Roaring Jack").[[37]](#cite_note-37) It has been particularly popular in the US and Canada, where there are large communities descended from Irish and Scottish immigrants. From the US this includes Irish-influenced bands [Flogging Molly](/wiki/Flogging_Molly "Flogging Molly"), [the Tossers](/wiki/The_Tossers "The Tossers"), [Dropkick Murphys](/wiki/Dropkick_Murphys "Dropkick Murphys"), [Street Dogs](/wiki/Street_Dogs "Street Dogs"), the Young Dubliners, [Black 47](/wiki/Black_47_(band) "Black 47 (band)"), [the Killdares](/wiki/The_Killdares "The Killdares"), [Flatfoot 56](/wiki/Flatfoot_56 "Flatfoot 56"), and [Jackdaw](/wiki/Jackdaw_(band) "Jackdaw (band)"), and Scottish bands such as [Seven Nations](/wiki/Seven_Nations_(band) "Seven Nations (band)"). From Canada come [the Dreadnoughts](/wiki/The_Dreadnoughts "The Dreadnoughts"),[[38]](#cite_note-38) [the Real McKenzies](/wiki/The_Real_McKenzies "The Real McKenzies") and [the Mahones](/wiki/The_Mahones "The Mahones"); from Australia, [the Rumjacks](/wiki/The_Rumjacks "The Rumjacks"),[[39]](#cite_note-39) Roaring Jack and [Mutiny](/wiki/Mutiny_(band) "Mutiny (band)"); Catgut Mary; from the UK, [Neck](/wiki/Neck_(band) "Neck (band)") (featuring a former member of Shane MacGowan's post-Pogues band, the Popes) and [Ferocious Dog](/wiki/Ferocious_Dog "Ferocious Dog"); from Germany, [Fiddler's Green](/wiki/Fiddler%27s_Green_(band) "Fiddler's Green (band)"); from the Czech Republic, [Pipes And Pints](/wiki/Pipes_And_Pints "Pipes And Pints"); and from Norway, [Greenland Whalefishers](/wiki/Greenland_Whalefishers "Greenland Whalefishers"). These groups were influenced by American forms of music, and sometimes contained members with no Celtic ancestry and had lyrics sung in English.[[40]](#cite_note-40)
